text: Hi, everyone. My name is Maxine. I am the co-founder and CTO of Forecast Bio. And thank you for the opportunity to speak here. It's really exciting for me to be a part of this overall community over the last little bit more than a year.

And I'm really excited about the possibilities that AtProto has for providing us agency over our social experience, over the way that we are able to shape our own science, our own investigation, and our own collective sensemaking, as I'm sure was talked about a lot at the AtScience meeting before the main meeting for this.

So I'm going to talk about what we're building at Forecast to facilitate this vision of a future of AI built on AtProto using the tools that are available built into the infrastructure for the protocol to be able to facilitate collective sharing and understanding of data sets, large data sets, and also of model weights.
So first, an introduction to Forecast, because it's probably a little bit out of left field to have a .bio domain show up at this setting, and not in the context of biographic, but actually biology.
So Forecast is a drug discovery and development company, actually.

And so our vision is really focused around providing people with cognitive agency and recognizing that historically we've done a very bad job of developing central nervous system therapeutics in the pharmaceutical space.

So what we want to do is to say, look, there is this vast space of where your mind could be at any given moment, and a vast way in which your mind can evolve in its instantaneous state across this space of all the different things that a mind can be.
So you're somewhere in this vast sea of all the potential mental states that are possible.
And what we want to do is to be able to produce therapeutic interventions.

We're starting with drugs, with small molecule drugs, but we'd really like to see this expand into all types of different interventions that allow you to say, okay, no matter which spot that you're at in this space,
I want to be able to move to any other spot in this space to give you control of the way that your mind operates over time.
And so we think that this overall vision provides a really different way of thinking about how drugs are developed in neurology and psychiatry,
where historically we've had a really difficult time bringing new tools to market.

And so we are using artificial intelligence models, both on the language side, on the image side, on the multimodal side, all of these different techniques that have really been radically transformative in every field of science and computation over the last few years,
to be able to build out this map of every cognitive state that a person can be in over time,

and then to understand the biology by growing many brains in the lab and then watching the videos of their brain activity over time to be able to map out how pharmaceutical interventions impact these things.
So the difficulty that we run into with this is that right now in the ecosystem for AI,
these models are really largely produced from a small subset of orgs, as I'm sure a lot of people in the audience are familiar with from this.
And this provides really a number of practical and, I think, for the field, for science as a whole, existential problems that come from,
like, what are the long-term impacts of having, like, the incentive structures that are present for individual large orgs producing these models,
the way that even just having any concentration of only a small, sparse subset of models makes people anchor their understanding.

like, say, in the biotech space, if you have image models that show you biological imaging,

And there's also, like, an outsized effect of, like, sort of quashing the variability in the entire ecosystem of the way investigation works.
